claimed_contribution: The paper shows that consumers are not indifferent across economically equivalent trade-in price formats and can prefer either overpayment or underpayment on the used model.
It develops a simple prospect-theory-based analytical model and provides experimental evidence for a critical used-to-new price ratio at which preference reverses.

It offers practical guidance for firms and policymakers on framing trade-ins and contributes to behavioral pricing by studying a naturally partitioned price with one gain component and one loss component.
context: The paper studies trade-in transactions in which consumers sell a used product while buying a new one, a common practice in durable-goods markets such as automobiles and electronics. The empirical tests use hypothetical retail digital camera trade-in scenarios with two stores offering different trade-in allowance/new-price splits but identical net outlay.
design_and_data: The authors first derive a formal analytical model from the prospect theory value function, emphasizing loss aversion and diminishing sensitivity, and define conditions for optimal over- versus underpayment framing. They then run three laboratory experiments with undergraduate participants using hypothetical camera trade-in offers that are financially equivalent but differently partitioned: Study 1 (n=55; plus a pretest n=52) manipulates low versus high used/new price ratios and reference-price information; Study 2 (n=94) manipulates low/high ratio and separate versus combined coding primes; Study 3 (n=188) manipulates regulatory focus (promotion vs prevention) and ratio under separate coding. Dependent measures include store choice, offer/store attractiveness ratings, and perceived separability checks.
main_findings: When the used-to-new price ratio is low, participants prefer the overpayment format, but when the ratio is high, preference reverses toward underpayment for economically equivalent deals.
The ratio-based reversal appears when consumers code the two price components separately; when the transaction is evaluated in combined form, the reversal is not observed.

Loss aversion moderates the effect: prevention-focused participants show the predicted reversal (overpayment at low ratio, underpayment at high ratio), whereas promotion-focused participants generally continue to prefer overpayment.
one_line_summary: Using a prospect-theory model and three experiments, the paper shows that preferences in economically equivalent trade-in deals switch from overpayment to underpayment as the used-to-new price ratio rises, especially when transactions are separately coded and loss aversion is stronger.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior trade-in research that emphasized a general preference for overpayment by identifying a ratio-dependent boundary condition and reversal to underpayment, and it links this to partitioned-pricing work by analyzing a mixed gain-loss partition rather than two expenditure components.
research_gap: Prior trade-in studies mainly document that consumers like being overpaid on the trade-in allowance in economically equivalent deals. The paper addresses the unresolved question of when this preference should reverse and what mechanism determines that tipping point.
research_question: Do consumers always prefer overpayment on a trade-in allowance in financially equivalent trade-in transactions, or can they prefer underpayment under some conditions? Specifically, does preference depend on the ratio of used-model price to new-model price, and on whether consumers code the two monetary components separately with loss aversion and diminishing sensitivity shaping the outcome?
